model.label: Claude Sonnet 4.6
model.description: Anthropic • Fast flagship Claude model for coding, analysis, and high-throughput agent use.

pick.rationale: Iowa State enters as the #2 seed and one of the nation's elite programs, ranked 7th in the AP Poll with a 27-7 record, an SRS of 27.15 (5th nationally), and the 6th-best defense in the country (DRtg 95.6) built against elite Big 12 competition — a level of two-way excellence that Tennessee State's OVC schedule (SOS ranked 342nd nationally) never approached. The efficiency gap is staggering: Iowa State's SRS of +27.15 versus Tennessee State's -4.54 represents a roughly 32-point chasm, with the Tigers' offense ranked 136th nationally running directly into the Cyclones' top-6 defense. Tennessee State is a feel-good story making their first NCAA Tournament appearance in 32 years under first-year coach Nolan Smith, but their 23-9 record was compiled almost entirely against weak mid-major competition and no credible upset path exists against a top-10 program with elite two-way efficiency. The historical 2-vs-15 record (~94% win rate for 2-seeds) and this specific efficiency chasm make Iowa State's advancement a near-certainty.

reasoningStep.title: Iowa State (#2) vs Tennessee State (#15) — Round of 64 Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Iowa State is a dominant #2 seed with elite two-way efficiency (top-6 defense, top-21 offense nationally) and a 27-7 Big 12 record. Tennessee State is a first-time tournament qualifier in 32 years with a negative SRS (-4.54, 216th nationally) and a schedule ranked 342nd in strength. The ~32-point SRS gap, Iowa State's 6th-ranked defense against Tennessee State's 136th-ranked offense, and the historical 2-vs-15 record all point overwhelmingly to Iowa State.
evidence: Iowa State: 27-7 record, 3rd in Big 12 (12-6), AP Poll #7, SRS 27.15 (5th nationally), SOS 10.50 (42nd), ORtg 120.0 (21st nationally), DRtg 95.6 (6th nationally)
Tennessee State: 23-9 record, 1st in OVC (15-5), SRS -4.54 (216th nationally), SOS -8.68 (342nd nationally), ORtg 111.5 (136th nationally), DRtg 101.5 (56th nationally)
Tennessee State is the #15 seed making their first NCAA Tournament appearance in 32 years under first-year coach Nolan Smith; defeated Morehead State 93-67 in OVC Championship
Iowa State confirmed as #2 seed in Midwest Region with analysts noting a favorable draw; no significant injury concerns reported heading into the tournament
Historical 2-vs-15 seed record: 2-seeds hold approximately a 94% all-time win rate in the modern NCAA Tournament era, and the efficiency gap here is among the largest in the bracket

result.text: Ticket Punched: Previewing the Tennessee State Tigers for the 2026 NCAA Tournament Articles Podcasts Scout Team Game Analysis Bracketology Women's Hoops Free Content Team Previews Scoreboard Search Sign Up Sign Up Create Your Account Get access to exclusive features and stay updated. Sign Up Sign Up Already have an account? Log In Articles Podcasts Scout Team Game Analysis Bracketology Women's Hoops Free Content Team Previews Scoreboard Become a member Become a member Home Articles & Editorials Ticket Punched: Previewing the Tennessee State Tigers for the 2026 NCAA Tournament Articles & Editorials Articles & Editorials Bracketology Bracketology Free Content Free Content Ticket Punched: Previewing the Tennessee State Tigers for the 2026 NCAA Tournament Tennessee State earned its first trip to the NCAA Tournament in 32 years by knocking off Morehead State in the OVC Championship Game. Featured Mar 8, 2026 by Lukas Harkins Not many first-year head coaches have had as successful years as Nolan Smith is in the midst of at Tennessee State... The former Duke standout and Portland Trail Blazer took over as head coach of the Tigers in July of this past offseason after stints as an assistant coach at each of Duke, Louisville, and Memphis, but didn't let the late start slow him down. Tennessee State lost each of its first three Division I games, but found some momentum heading into conference play before co-winning the regular-season Ohio Valley title with a 15-5 record. Smith was named the OVC's Co-Coach of the Year, while Aaron Nkrumah was named the league's Player of the Year. As the No. 1 overall seed at the OVC Tournament, the Tigers earned a bye to the semifinals, where they knocked off UT Martin by 13 points. The ensuing evening, they pounced on Morehead State from the opening tip with a 3-point onslaught. Despite having entered the game ranked 350th in 3-point attempt rate and only making 12+ 3s in one regular-season game, Tennessee State shot 12-for-15 from beyond the arc in the first half of the title game en route to a 21-point advantage. Fast-forward to the final buzzer, and Tennessee State won the league's automatic bid with a 93-67 score. While the Tigers' best 3-point performance of the season was one of the top stories from the title game, making 16-of-26 attempts, their perimeter shooting wasn't what got them to this point. Now an NCAA Tournament team for the first time in 32 years, let's take a deeper look at Tennessee State. Tennessee State has dominated the possession battle. Narrowing the focus to Tennessee State's league games, its offense was superb. The Tigers finished in the top 4 of the league in the majority of important statistics, including leading the conference in effective field goal percentage and turnover rate. Tennessee State's raw offensive efficiency of 114.8 of league games led the league by 2.6 points. While the defense only ranked 10th in effective field goal percentage allowed, it forced turnovers and limit

Log In Articles Podcasts Scout Team Game Analysis Bracketology Women's Hoops Free Content Team Previews Scoreboard Become a member Become a member Home Articles & Editorials Ticket Punched: Previewing the Tennessee State Tigers for the 2026 NCAA Tournament Articles & Editorials Articles & Editorials Bracketology Bracketology Free Content Free Content Ticket Punched: Previewing the Tennessee State Tigers for the 2026 NCAA Tournament Tennessee State earned its first trip to the NCAA Tournament in 32 years by knocking off Morehead State in the OVC Championship Game. Featured Mar 8, 2026 by Lukas Harkins Not many first-year head coaches have had as successful years as Nolan Smith is in the midst of at Tennessee State... The former Duke standout and Portland Trail Blazer took over as head coach of the Tigers in July of this past offseason after stints as an assistant coach at each of Duke, Louisville, and Memphis, but didn't let the late start slow him down. Tennessee State lost each of its first three Division I games, but found some momentum heading into conference play before co-winning the regular-season Ohio Valley title with a 15-5 record. Smith was named the OVC's Co-Coach of the Year, while Aaron Nkrumah was named the league's Player of the Year. As the No. 1 overall seed at the OVC Tournament, the Tigers earned a bye to the semifinals, where they knocked off UT Martin by 13 points. The ensuing evening, they pounced on Morehead State from the opening tip with a 3-point onslaught. Despite having entered the game ranked 350th in 3-point attempt rate and only making 12+ 3s in one regular-season game, Tennessee State shot 12-for-15 from beyond the arc in the first half of the title game en route to a 21-point advantage. Fast-forward to the final buzzer, and Tennessee State won the league's automatic bid with a 93-67 score. While the Tigers' best 3-point performance of the season was one of the top stories from the title game, making 16-of-26 attempts, their perimeter shooting wasn't what got them to this point. Now an NCAA Tournament team for the first time in 32 years, let's take a deeper look at Tennessee State. Tennessee State has dominated the possession battle. Narrowing the focus to Tennessee State's league games, its offense was superb. The Tigers finished in the top 4 of the league in the majority of important statistics, including leading the conference in effective field goal percentage and turnover rate. Tennessee State's raw offensive efficiency of 114.8 of league games led the league by 2.6 points. While the defense only ranked 10th in effective field goal percentage allowed, it forced turnovers and limit

modelTrace.content:
I now have sufficient data to make a well-informed pick. Let me compile what I know:

**Iowa State (2-seed):**
- Record: 27-7, 3rd in Big 12 (12-6)
- AP Poll: 7th nationally
- SRS: 27.15 (5th nationally)
- SOS: 10.50 (42nd nationally)
- ORtg: 120.0 (21st nationally)
- DRtg: 95.6 (6th nationally — elite defense)
- No significant injury concerns flagged for the 2026 tournament

**Tennessee State (15-seed):**
- Record: 23-9, 1st in OVC (15-5) — OVC Regular Season AND Tournament Champion
- SRS: -4.54 (216th nationally)
- SOS: -8.68 (342nd nationally — extremely weak schedule)
- ORtg: 111.5 (136th nationally)
- DRtg: 101.5 (56th nationally — actually decent defense)
- First NCAA Tournament appearance in 32 years
- First-year coach Nolan Smith (former Duke player)

**Key matchup facts:**
- Iowa State is the #2 seed vs. #15 seed Tennessee State
- Iowa State's SRS advantage: ~31.7 points (27.15 vs. -4.54)
- Iowa State's defense (6th nationally, DRtg 95.6) vs. Tennessee State's offense (136th nationally, ORtg 111.5) is a severe mismatch
- Tennessee State's SOS ranked 342nd — OVC schedule provides no preparation for Big 12-caliber competition
- Historical 2-vs-15 record: 2-seeds win ~94% of the time
- No credible upset path for Tennessee State given the efficiency gap

The efficiency gap between these teams is staggering: Iowa State's SRS of +27.15 versus Tennessee State's -4.54 represents a roughly 32-point chasm, with the Tigers' offense ranked 136th nationally running directly into the Cyclones' top-6 defense. Tennessee State is a feel-good story — their first NCAA Tournament appearance in 32 years under first-year coach Nolan Smith — but their 23-9 record was compiled almost entirely against weak mid-major competition, and no credible upset path exists for a team ranked 216th nationally against a top-10 program with elite two-way efficiency.
